title: Source lineage for aje0623 / The Wilton Chronicle
summary: aje0623 is the raw 2006 University of Michigan Level-1, uncorrected-OCR representation of Horstmann's title page and Middle English poem on printed pp. 1-111. The repository artifact, shared U-M item, abstract Wilton Chronicle, Horstmann edition and frame, sole physical Cotton witness, later proofed/restructured/expanded phase-3 CME00147 artifact, and complete edition and manuscript facsimiles remain distinct. Empty page placeholders for printed pp. 112-116 do not import the Latin appendices into aje0623 content.

entities.label: General Corpus raw repository artifact for aje0623
entities.description: The checked-in phase-1/2 XML preservation of the U-M AJE0623.0001.001 export: 2006 Level-1 TEI-in-Libraries markup generated from uncorrected OCR, with title page, poem, notes, and explicit omitted-page placeholders.
notes: The raw header LANGUAGE value ger reflects Horstmann's editorial frame, not the intrinsic language of the Middle English target work.
The file contains 1,245 LG and 4,988 L; five L rows are non-verbal display or lacuna rows, 4,983 contain extant verbal verse, and conventional lineation has 4,986 positions.
The folio milestones omit 215 and 220 and duplicate 222; this is a representation anomaly, not a physical refoliation.

entities.label: General Corpus repository artifact for CME00147
entities.description: The distinct later phase-3 repository XML, with a 2016 TEMPHEAD documenting proofing, structural/tag/page-break review, added HI markup, and corrections. It represents the same U-M/Horstmann base as aje0623 but restructures the poem and expands content through printed p. 112 to include Nomina fundatorum and the first seven Auctoritates entries.
notes: This artifact is not same_as the raw aje0623 artifact or the current U-M HTML item.
It has 4,986 L elements: 4,983 verbal rows and three major-lacuna display rows; it uses 3 LG wrappers, 6,738 HI spans, and 113 PB.
Its expanded Latin content remains outside aje0623 target content and arithmetic.

entities.label: University of Michigan electronic text AJE0623.0001.001
alternate_labels: S. Editha, sive Chronicon vilodunense im Wiltshire dialekt, aus Ms. Cotton. Faustina B III
entities.description: The shared U-M Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item whose raw 2006 export is preserved by aje0623. It represents Horstmann's title page, an unnumbered body page, the complete poem on printed pp. 1-111, and empty placeholders for omitted preliminaries and printed pp. 112-116.
notes: The U-M catalog places Edith, Saint in an author field. The anonymous medieval poet remains unidentified; Edith is the subject.
The current HTML item is publicly readable. Historical page-image links are advertised, but the full 124-image sequence was not re-audited because automated page requests returned HTTP 403.
The raw XML's 2006 generic copyright/restriction warning is retained separately from the current NoC-US assertion.

entities.label: The Wilton Chronicle
entities.description: Anonymous Middle English verse hagiography and institutional history composed around 1420 for or in close association with Wilton Abbey. It integrates Wilton's royal foundations and historical memory with the life, relic translation, miracles, and cult of St Edith.

date_statements.label: composition
date_statements.value: around 1420, probably during the reign of Henry V
date_statements.notes: The accompanying founders list ends with Henry V as the reigning king; the normalized range is an inference, not a dated colophon.

notes: Only one manuscript witness is known.
The author is anonymous and closely associated with Wilton. Chaplain, priest, or nun identities remain hypotheses.
The poem is incomplete because a twelve-folio gathering was removed after conventional line 1030.
The adjacent Latin appendices are not constituent content of aje0623.

entities.label: Legenda sanctae Edithae
entities.description: The eleventh-century Latin Edith legend associated with Goscelin and treated in modern scholarship as a principal source for the Wilton poem's hagiographic material. The exact recension or physical exemplar used by the Middle English poet has not been identified.
notes: Horstmann questioned whether the short twelve-chapter Vita printed by the Bollandists was identical with the Legenda cited by the poet.

entities.label: Horstmann's 1883 edition of S. Editha
alternate_labels: S. Editha sive Chronicon Vilodunense im Wiltshire Dialekt aus Ms. Cotton. Faustina B III
entities.description: Carl Horstmann's complete scholarly volume, including German introduction, a direct transcription of the Cotton witness, extensive critical and source notes, Latin appendices, remarks, and a second appendix. aje0623 represents the title page and poem on printed pp. 1-111; its PB placeholders explicitly omit printed pp. 112-116.

bibliographic.citation: Carl Horstmann, ed., S. Editha sive Chronicon Vilodunense im Wiltshire Dialekt aus Ms. Cotton. Faustina B III (Heilbronn: Gebr. Henninger, 1883).
bibliographic.title: S. Editha sive Chronicon Vilodunense im Wiltshire Dialekt aus Ms. Cotton. Faustina B III

notes: Horstmann cites Black's 1830 edition as generally correct but repeatedly reports manuscript readings, corrections, erasures, and differences from Black, demonstrating direct recollation rather than derivation from Black alone.
Printed pp. 111-113 contain adjacent Latin material after the poem; only the poem portion of p. 111 is target content in aje0623.

entities.label: Black's 1830 first edition of Chronicon Vilodunense
alternate_labels: Chronicon Vilodunense; sive de vita et miraculis Sanctae Edithae
entities.description: William Henry Black's first printed edition of the unique Cotton text, produced at the expense of Richard Colt Hoare. Horstmann describes it as generally correct and uses it as a collation point while returning to the manuscript.

bibliographic.citation: William Henry Black, ed., Chronicon Vilodunense; sive de vita et miraculis Sanctae Edithae Regis Edgari filiae carmen vetus Anglicum, e codice unico Cottoniano (London: Typis Nicholsianis, 1830).
bibliographic.title: Chronicon Vilodunense; sive de vita et miraculis Sanctae Edithae Regis Edgari filiae carmen vetus Anglicum, e codice unico Cottoniano

notes: A current British Library component bibliography gives 1802, but the inspected Horstmann history, DIMEV, title citation, and library holdings support 1830.
Only 100 copies were reportedly printed; no complete public digital scan was located in the searches recorded here.

entities.label: London, British Library, Cotton MS Faustina B III, complete composite codex
alternate_labels: Cotton MS Faustina B III, ff. 199-280
entities.description: The complete composite Cotton codex serving as the physical carrier for the sole witness of The Wilton Chronicle. The target poem is current ff. 199r-263r; adjacent Latin Wilton material follows on ff. 263r-264v, and the companion Middle English Life of St Æthelthryth follows on ff. 265r-279v. The target component was produced at Wilton in the first quarter of the fifteenth century.

date_statements.label: target component production
date_statements.value: first quarter of the fifteenth century

date_statements.label: probable copying date in modern scholarship

historical_designations: Horstmann's foliation ff. 194-259 for the poem and appendices
holding.notes: The complete physical carrier is Cotton MS Faustina B III. Current foliation is five leaves higher than Horstmann's milestones across the target component; the poem is current ff. 199r-263r.

physical_description.extent: Composite codex: i + 280 folios plus modern paper flyleaves; target poem ff. 199r-263r. The provider facsimile exposes front/back boards, endleaves and blanks, ff. 1r-280v, and spine.
notes: The poem has a twelve-folio gathering missing after conventional line 1030, between Horstmann old ff. 205 and 206 / current ff. 210 and 211.
Benskin treats the extant language as mixed and predominantly West Midland, produced by a scribe probably from northern Warwickshire or southern Staffordshire and likely working at Wilton.
The same hand copies the Edith poem, intervening Latin, and Æthelthryth poem; this does not establish autograph status.

entities.label: Internet Archive complete digital facsimile of Horstmann's 1883 volume
entities.description: A complete 136-page scan of a University of Toronto copy, with PDF, OCR text, and Internet Archive metadata. The aje0623 target poem maps to printed pp. 1-111 / PDF pp. 15-125; the encoded title page is PDF p. 7.
entities.reproduction_format: 136-page PDF, OCR text, and item metadata
notes: The complete volume is cached, including the German introduction and Latin/back matter omitted as text by aje0623.

entities.label: British Library complete IIIF facsimile of Cotton MS Faustina B III
entities.description: The provider-published 577-canvas color IIIF sequence for the complete Cotton MS Faustina B III composite carrier, from front board through spine. The aje0623 target poem occupies current ff. 199r-263r / canvases 407-535.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation manifest with 577 color image canvases; local bundle requests 568 images at 1800-pixel width and retains 9 native-size fallbacks
notes: Complete means every canvas exposed by the provider manifest when retrieved, including boards, endleaves, blanks, the complete folio sequence, and spine; it does not assert independent audit of unexposed physical surfaces.
The provider manifest states: Public Domain in most countries other than the UK. UK and contractual questions are modeled separately in rights.
The complete carrier facsimile is not reduced to the target work portion; target presence is localized separately in access.local_copies.

entities.label: DIMEV record 421, Almighty God in Trinity
entities.description: The Digital Index of Middle English Verse record for The Life of St Editha, with incipit, explicit, sole-witness locus, current foliation note, IMEV/NIMEV number 243, and Black and Horstmann edition references.

entities.label: Dockray-Miller's 2009 edition and translation
alternate_labels: Saints Edith and Æthelthryth: Princesses, Miracle Workers, and their Late Medieval Audience
entities.description: Modern scholarly edition, facing or accompanying translation, notes, glossary, and study of The Wilton Chronicle and the companion Wilton Life of St Æthelthryth.

bibliographic.citation: Mary Dockray-Miller, ed. and trans., Saints Edith and Æthelthryth: Princesses, Miracle Workers, and their Late Medieval Audience: The Wilton Chronicle and the Wilton Life of St Æthelthryth, Medieval Women: Texts and Contexts 25 (Turnhout: Brepols, 2009).
bibliographic.title: Saints Edith and Æthelthryth: Princesses, Miracle Workers, and their Late Medieval Audience: The Wilton Chronicle and the Wilton Life of St Æthelthryth

notes: This later edition supports interpretation and comparison but is not upstream of the 1883 source represented by aje0623.

entities.label: Benskin's 1991 dialect and scribal study
entities.description: Michael Benskin's complete article 'In reply to Dr Burton,' especially its analysis of the Cotton Faustina B III Wilton component. It separates likely South Wiltshire originals from a mixed, predominantly West Midland copy and distinguishes author from scribe.

bibliographic.citation: Michael Benskin, 'In reply to Dr Burton,' Leeds Studies in English, new series 22 (1991): 209-262.
bibliographic.title: In reply to Dr Burton

entities.label: Camp's study of Edith and women's history
entities.description: Cynthia Turner Camp's chapter analyzing the poem's reconstruction of Wilton's pre-Conquest past for a female community and its joining of saints' life with history writing.

bibliographic.citation: Cynthia Turner Camp, 'Edith of Wilton and the Writing of Women's History,' in Anglo-Saxon Saints' Lives as History Writing in Late Medieval England (Boydell & Brewer, 2015), 25-63.
bibliographic.title: Edith of Wilton and the Writing of Women's History
bibliographic.container_title: Anglo-Saxon Saints' Lives as History Writing in Late Medieval England

entities.label: British Library digitisation services and 2025/26 rate card
entities.description: Current British Library guidance and price schedule for ordering research JPEGs or reproduction-quality TIFF images. It is an access aid, not a facsimile of the manuscript.

agents.name: Richard Colt Hoare
notes: Funded Black's 1830 publication.

agents.name: Digital Index of Middle English Verse
Goscelin of Saint-Bertin
notes: Associated with the Latin Edith legend, not the Middle English poem.

object.description: The U-M AJE0623.0001.001 electronic item as represented by its 2006 export.

scope.notes: The relation records source identity and export lineage; it does not claim byte identity with the current HTML serialization.

subject.description: Complete later phase-3 XML, including its proofed/restructured poem and added Latin selection.

scope.notes: A later proofed, restructured, and expanded repository expression of the same U-M/Horstmann base; the two artifacts have non-identical structure and content extent and are never same_as.

assertion.notes: Direction is supported by pinned phase directories, the 2016 revision header, shared text, and structural/file comparison; the header does not name a byte-for-byte conversion workflow.

subject.description: Title page, unnumbered body page, poem, and explicit omitted-page placeholders in the U-M item.

object.description: Horstmann title page and poem printed pp. 1-111; preliminaries and printed pp. 112-116 are represented only as omissions.
The target poem within the full composite carrier.

subject.description: The sole surviving manuscript copy of the poem.

object.description: An unidentified recension or exemplar of the Latin Edith legend, not securely the short Bollandist Vita.

assertion.notes: This is a source-work relation, not identification of a physical exemplar or direct attribution of the English poem to Goscelin.

scope.notes: The facsimile edge maps the same full carrier surfaces; target presence remains ff. 199r-263r / canvases 407-535 in the cached-bundle work_portion.

Repository checkout, pinned GitHub blob, or pinned Git object

access.format: Public HTML full text with advertised page-view routes
notes: The full text and item record are public.
The header says 124 linked page images, but automated current page-image requests returned HTTP 403; current complete image availability was not asserted.

access.format: Complete 136-page PDF, complete OCR text, and item metadata

work_portion.label: The Wilton Chronicle inside the complete Horstmann volume
notes: The title page at PDF p. 7 is representation frame; the abstract target work is the poem on printed pp. 1-111.
Printed p. 111 ends the poem. Latin material beginning later on that page and continuing on pp. 112-116 is outside aje0623 target content.

work_portion.label: OCR stream for The Wilton Chronicle
locators: poem printed pp. 1-111 within the complete-volume OCR
notes: Machine OCR supports searching; page images and the raw XML remain the reproducible boundary evidence.

IIIF Presentation manifest and color JPEG images

notes: ZIP contains the provider manifest, exact-source inventory, and all 577 canvas images exposed when retrieved.
568 images were requested at 1800-pixel width; 9 canvases used available native full-size fallbacks.

work_portion.label: The Wilton Chronicle within the complete composite-codex bundle
notes: Canvas 535/f. 263r contains the poem ending above and adjacent Latin below; only the poem portion belongs to aje0623.
The complete 577-canvas carrier is cached without cropping to the target.

local_copies.label: Cotton MS Faustina B III IIIF manifest
notes: This metadata file is not itself a manuscript facsimile; the ZIP contains the complete image sequence.
The component catalog's legacy images-unavailable sentence is superseded in practice by the current parent record's full digital coverage and this working manifest.
The facsimile access object is complete-carrier access; the local-copy work_portion separately marks ff. 199r-263r / canvases 407-535.

access.access_method: Use the British Library Archives and Manuscripts collection-item request form with a current Reader Pass. The catalog states that a letter of introduction is required and consultation is onsite in London, subject to current conditions.

Submit the online digitisation form with shelfmark Cotton MS Faustina B III and exact folios. The service distinguishes reference JPEGs from reproduction-quality TIFFs and asks users to contact licensing for publication reuse.
access.format: Research JPEG or reproduction-quality TIFF quotation
notes: The public IIIF sequence already provides a complete research facsimile. A paid order may be useful only if higher resolution, color control, or publication-quality files are needed.

The rate card lists 2025/26 standard charges of £15.25 for the first JPEG image and £0.58 for each subsequent JPEG; £27.70 for the first TIFF and £7.25 for each subsequent TIFF; complex imaging is £58.19 per image. Obtain a current quotation before relying on these rates.

local_copies.label: British Library digitisation rate card based on 2025/26 rates

notes: The current BL digitisation page still links this PDF, which says prices are based on 2025/26 rates; obtain a current quotation.

access.format: HTML record and complete XML source release

access.access_method: No complete public digital copy was located. The University of Leeds Special Collections catalog reports an Archive Print copy at Large Modern History Y-3.1/WIL; consult Leeds for onsite access or reproduction possibilities.

notes: The publisher listed €95 excluding VAT when checked on 2026-07-16; price and availability may change.

access.format: Public chapter abstract and bibliographic page; full text availability depends on current access

rights.component: Raw aje0623 repository XML, markup, and preserved OCR representation
rights.basis: The pinned CME repository contains the Unlicense text framed as applying to software. Its scope over corpus XML, OCR-derived text, markup, data, and edition-derived typography is not explicit enough to treat the full artifact as unambiguously licensed.
notes: Underlying medieval text, historical editions, manuscript images, and modern scholarship are separately scoped.

rights.component: Later phase-3 repository XML, markup, correction, and revision metadata
rights.basis: The same pinned repository Unlicense is framed for software and does not unambiguously state its scope over the phase-3 XML, markup, data, proofing changes, or edition-derived typography.

rights.component: Electronic text as distributed by the University of Michigan
rights.basis: The current U-M item explicitly states that the material is in the public domain and supplies the No Copyright-United States rights statement. This record uses public_domain for that exact United States assertion, not the distinct no_known_copyright concept.
notes: NoC-US is jurisdiction-specific and is not generalized into a worldwide determination.
The raw 2006 generic warning is preserved in a separate rights record.

rights.component: Generic availability warning embedded in the 2006 raw XML export
rights.basis: The raw header states that copyright may apply where applicable and that other distribution restrictions may apply. It is retained as a dated generic provider warning, separate from the current item-specific NoC-US assertion.
notes: This historical generic statement does not override or broaden the current item-specific United States public-domain assertion.

rights.basis: The anonymous work was composed around 1420 and is centuries outside copyright term. Modern editions, markup, translations, scholarship, and photographs remain separate components.

rights.component: Fifteenth-century manuscript textual and codicological content
rights.basis: The manuscript content is medieval and centuries outside copyright term. Physical ownership, photography, and provider terms are separate.

rights.component: 1883 edition, transcription, introduction, notes, and typography
rights.basis: The volume was published in 1883 and is outside the maximum United States copyright term; Internet Archive metadata marks the item NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT in the United States.

rights.jurisdiction: United States and jurisdictions with ordinary author-term protection
rights.basis: The edition was published in 1830 and its nineteenth-century editor died long ago; the work is outside ordinary copyright terms. Repository or reproduction terms for a particular copy remain separate.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan derivatives and item access
rights.basis: Internet Archive item metadata records possible-copyright-status NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT and copyright-region US.

rights.jurisdiction: Most countries other than the United Kingdom, as asserted by the provider
rights.basis: The IIIF manifest's item-level rights field states Public Domain in most countries other than the UK. The provider does not enumerate every covered jurisdiction, and users remain responsible for their determination.
notes: This is an attributed provider statement, not a Creative Commons license or a universal worldwide determination.
The British Library terms say content specifically marked public domain may be used as desired but place responsibility and risk on the user.

rights.basis: The item-level IIIF statement expressly excludes the UK from its broad public-domain claim. No item-specific UK open license was found.
notes: Ask the British Library for written guidance before redistributing the complete image bundle or using photographs in UK-facing commercial publication.

rights.component: Newly commissioned research or reproduction-quality images
rights.basis: The imaging service applies its own order terms and directs publication users to licensing. A paid image order does not by itself grant redistribution or publication rights.
The versioned source release includes a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 license.

rights.component: Modern edition, translation, notes, glossary, and scholarship
rights.basis: The edition was published in 2009 and is currently sold by its publisher; no open-content license was found.
The complete modern article is publicly downloadable, but no Creative Commons or equivalent open-reuse license was found on the landing page or PDF.
notes: Facts and short attributed analysis may be cited; public download is not treated as permission to republish the article wholesale.

rights.basis: The underlying Latin legend is medieval and centuries outside copyright term. Modern editions, translations, scholarship, and digital representations remain separately protected.

rights.component: Modern 2015 scholarly chapter and publisher page
rights.basis: The modern chapter is in a commercially published scholarly monograph; no open-content license was found.
The rate card is current institutional service content and no item-specific open-content license is printed in the PDF; the BL site-level text license is not generalized to the PDF.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Complete Horstmann volume with title page, introduction pp. III-VIII, poem pp. 1-111, and back matter pp. 112-116
editorial_practices.representation: The raw export encodes the title page and uncorrected OCR for the poem on pp. 1-111, while preliminaries and pp. 112-116 are page placeholders without text.
editorial_practices.description: The header explicitly says OCR was not corrected and content was not edited, using Level 1 TEI-in-Libraries recommendations. The electronic item is complete for the target poem but selective relative to the complete edition.
editorial_practices.scope: Entire raw U-M item relative to Horstmann 1883

editorial_practices.source_feature: Cotton manuscript readings and Black's 1830 first edition
editorial_practices.representation: Horstmann transcribes the manuscript directly, describes Black as generally correct, and repeatedly records manuscript corrections, erasures, uncertain readings, and Black variants.
editorial_practices.description: Black is a comparison edition; Cotton Faustina B III is Horstmann's base source.
editorial_practices.scope: The Middle English poem and its apparatus

editorial_practices.source_feature: Raw U-M/Horstmann-derived encoding, printed proof sheets, title page, structure, page breaks, damage and typography
editorial_practices.representation: The 2016 TEMPHEAD reports proofing and tag review, added HI spans, moved PBs, corrections, and structural changes; file comparison also shows 3 LG wrappers, 4,986 L, and added Latin through p. 112.
editorial_practices.description: The phase-3 artifact is a later proofed, restructured, and expanded expression. Its changes support a directional version relation but do not make it identical to aje0623.

evidence.citation: CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/aje0623.xml at pinned CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: Primary raw artifact evidence for file identity, U-M/DLPS identifiers, 2006 publication and generic warning, uncorrected OCR policy, title page, target poem, omitted-page placeholders, notes, and milestone/markup behavior.

evidence.citation: Local XML/XPath audit of aje0623.xml.
evidence.summary: The well-formed file has 3 DIV1, 7 P, 124 unique sequential PB, 125 EPB, 4,988 L, 1,245 LG, 364 NOTE1, 363 HI1, 64 folio MILESTONE, 4 GAP, and 2 ABBR. Five L rows are non-verbal display/lacuna rows; 4,983 contain extant verbal verse and conventional lineation has 4,986 positions. Title/page mapping confirms no Latin back-matter text is encoded.

evidence.citation: CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00147.xml at pinned CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.locator: TEMPHEAD revision record; title page; poem; addendum through printed p. 112
evidence.summary: Distinct later artifact evidence. Its 2016-08-22 revision record documents proofing, structural and tag review, moved PBs, added HIs, and corrections. The file has Git blob 06b50c43f87dfc0a57ab3e17dac596b89688b326 and SHA-256 79024e3412a8615adcb7bfcdb90959d27952ed6bbe890e9c03efe696359c9ba8.

evidence.citation: Pinned-tree and XML comparison of phase-1/2 aje0623.xml with phase-3 CME00147.xml.
evidence.summary: Confirms the same Horstmann title and 4,983 verbal verse rows while distinguishing structures and extent: raw AJE has 1,245 LG, 4,988 L, 363 HI1, and omitted back-matter placeholders; phase-3 has 3 LG, 4,986 L, 6,738 HI, 113 PB, and substantive Latin through p. 112. This supports the later directional version relation but not same_as.

evidence.citation: CME/LICENSE at pinned commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: Contains the Unlicense text framed as applying to software; it does not explicitly resolve scope over XML, markup, data, historical editions, or manuscript images.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, S. Editha, sive Chronicon vilodunense im Wiltshire dialekt, AJE0623.0001.001.
evidence.summary: Official current item page confirming AJE0623.0001.001, Horstmann's 1883 title and publication, public HTML full text, contents divisions for omitted front matter / S. Editha / omitted back matter, and the exact public-domain / No Copyright-United States assertion.
notes: The catalog's Edith-as-author display is not transferred to medieval authorship.
The raw 2006 generic restriction warning is retained separately from the current item-specific NoC-US statement.

evidence.citation: Carl Horstmann, ed., S. Editha sive Chronicon Vilodunense im Wiltshire Dialekt aus Ms. Cotton. Faustina B III (Heilbronn: Gebr. Henninger, 1883).
evidence.locator: complete 136-page scan; title page; introduction pp. III-VIII; target poem printed pp. 1-111 / PDF pp. 15-125; adjacent back matter pp. 111-116
evidence.summary: Complete edition evidence for title, publication, Black 1830, direct Cotton transcription and collation, target poem boundary, conventional 4,986-position lineation, lacuna, old foliation, author/date hypotheses, and adjacent material explicitly omitted as text by aje0623.
notes: The hash is for the complete locally cached PDF.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive metadata for sedithsivechroni00edituoft.
evidence.summary: Records the 1883 volume, University of Toronto source copy, 136 scan images, and possible-copyright-status NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT for the United States.
notes: The hash is for the exact cached 2026-07-13 metadata JSON response.
A live 2026-07-16 response differed only in dynamic envelope fields (response creation time and server order); substantive item metadata and the 26-file count were unchanged.

evidence.citation: British Library, Cotton MS Faustina B III, Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue record 040-001103878.
evidence.locator: parent codex and contents, especially ff. 199r-279v
evidence.summary: Current institutional parent record for shelfmark, composite codex, parchment support, current foliation and contents, full digital coverage, IIIF LARK, and physical-access procedure.

evidence.citation: British Library, Cotton MS Faustina B III, ff. 199-280, Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue record 041-001103881.
evidence.locator: ff. 199r-263r Life of St Edith; ff. 263r-264v Latin chronicle of Wilton; ff. 265r-279v Life of St Æthelthryth
evidence.summary: Current component description supporting the first-quarter-fifteenth-century date, Wilton origin, Middle English verse-life locus, adjacent Latin text, and companion Æthelthryth poem.
notes: The record retains a legacy images-unavailable statement despite the current parent record and working IIIF service.
Its bibliography gives Black's edition year as 1802; other direct evidence supports 1830.

evidence.citation: British Library, IIIF Presentation manifest for Cotton MS Faustina B III, ark:/81055/vdc_100060431849.0x000001.
evidence.summary: Working provider manifest enumerating 577 canvases for the complete composite carrier, supplying exact Image API requests and the item-level statement Public Domain in most countries other than the UK. The local ZIP contains all 577 images plus manifest and inventory.
notes: The SHA-256 on this evidence record is for the complete locally cached IIIF ZIP; the separately cached live-matching manifest JSON has SHA-256 6276d335bca9315721a03b9108d8b2688d897753f0cd5be457f5c70fefce180b.
The ZIP passed integrity validation and inventory checks; target presence is localized to images 000407-000535.

evidence.citation: Visual inspection of Cotton MS Faustina B III in the British Library IIIF sequence and complete local bundle.
evidence.locator: target poem current ff. 199r-263r / canvases 407-535; complete carrier front through spine
evidence.summary: Visual inspection confirms f. 199r/canvas 407 as the target start and the poem ending in the upper part of f. 263r/canvas 535; adjacent Latin begins below on the same canvas. Front/back boards, endleaves/blanks, and spine samples confirm complete-provider-carrier coverage.

evidence.citation: British Library, Terms of Use.
evidence.summary: States that content specifically marked public domain is believed to be public domain in most territories and may be used as desired, while placing responsibility on users and applying broader terms to site and service use.

evidence.citation: British Library, Digitisation Services.
evidence.summary: Current order route and guidance for shelfmark, title and folio requests, reference JPEG versus reproduction-quality TIFF, manuscript photography, and licensing contact for publication reuse.

evidence.citation: British Library, digitisation price schedule generated 11 March 2026 and based on 2025/26 rates.
evidence.summary: Lists standard JPEG, TIFF, and complex-imaging charges and supports the fee status of new reproduction orders.
notes: The hash is for the complete locally cached rate-card PDF; users should request a current quotation.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, record 421, Almighty God in Trinity, version 1.0.
evidence.summary: Identifies title/description, incipit, explicit, sole Cotton witness at current ff. 199-263, and Black 1830 and Horstmann 1883 editions; the source release is licensed CC BY 4.0.
notes: The hash is for the complete locally cached DIMEV v1.0 ZIP release.

evidence.citation: Brepols, Mary Dockray-Miller, Saints Edith and Æthelthryth: Princesses, Miracle Workers, and their Late Medieval Audience (2009).
evidence.summary: Official publisher description for the modern edition and translation, conventional title The Wilton Chronicle, Wilton and early-fifteenth-century composition, bibliographic identifiers, contents, and current purchase access.

evidence.citation: Mechthild Gretsch, review of Saints Edith and Æthelthryth, English Historical Review 126, no. 518 (2011): 121-122.
evidence.summary: Independent scholarly review describing the poem's historical opening, the Edith life after the manuscript loss, likely early-fifteenth-century date, possible common authorship with the Æthelthryth poem, Wilton connection, and manuscript production around 1425.

evidence.citation: The Medieval Review, review of Mary Dockray-Miller, Saints Edith and Æthelthryth (2009).
evidence.summary: Summarizes the modern edition and argument that the poet relied primarily on the eleventh-century Goscelin-associated Legenda Edithae while reshaping it for Wilton, women readers, and joined historical-hagiographic purposes.

evidence.citation: Michael Benskin, 'In reply to Dr Burton,' Leeds Studies in English, new series 22 (1991): 209-262.
evidence.summary: Separates likely South Wiltshire originals from the mixed, predominantly West Midland extant copy; localizes the scribe toward northern Warwickshire or southern Staffordshire and probably Wilton; and distinguishes author, scribe, and institutional setting.
notes: The hash is for the complete locally cached 55-page article PDF.

evidence.citation: Cynthia Turner Camp, 'Edith of Wilton and the Writing of Women's History,' in Anglo-Saxon Saints' Lives as History Writing in Late Medieval England (Boydell & Brewer, 2015), 25-63.
evidence.summary: Frames the poem as an anonymous work composed around 1420 for an identifiable female community, reconstructing Wilton's pre-Conquest past through joined hagiography and history writing.

evidence.citation: University of Leeds Special Collections, participant/holding result for William Henry Black and Chronicon Vilodunense (1830).
evidence.summary: Reports a Leeds Archive Print holding of Black's 1830 edition at Large Modern History Y-3.1/WIL, providing a request or onsite lead when no public digital copy was located.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive advanced search for Chronicon Vilodunense and 1830, supplemented by title, editor, and imprint searches.
evidence.summary: No complete public digital copy of Black's 1830 edition was located in refreshed title, editor, imprint, and repository searches. The result is no public copy found, not lost or unavailable.

open_questions.question: Does a complete public digital facsimile of William Henry Black's scarce 1830 edition exist outside the repositories searched?
next_steps: Ask University of Leeds Special Collections about photography or digitisation of Large Modern History Y-3.1/WIL.
Search Google Books, HathiTrust, British Library, Bodleian, Salisbury Cathedral, and WorldCat holdings by the full Latin title and imprint.
Cache the complete edition if a lawful direct download becomes available.
notes: The edition is extant and requestable through holdings; no-public-copy-found is only a digital-access finding.

open_questions.question: What exact source or editorial function does each of aje0623's 363 HI1 spans serve, and how do those spans relate to the later phase-3 artifact's 6,738 HI spans?
next_steps: Collate the raw file's 354 superscript and 9 italic HI1 spans against Horstmann and the manuscript.
Compare phase-3 additions separately; do not import its denser markup into aje0623.
notes: The available facsimile makes this research practical, but the present lineage does not equate every italic span with one kind of elision.

open_questions.question: Which recension and physical exemplar of the Latin Edith legend underlies the Middle English poem?
next_steps: Compare Dockray-Miller's full source apparatus with Horstmann's objections to identifying the named Legenda with the short Bollandist Vita.
Do not create a direct physical-exemplar relation without manuscript or edition evidence.

open_questions.question: What written reuse determination does the British Library apply to redistribution or publication of these IIIF images in the United Kingdom and in a commercial print-on-demand context?
next_steps: Ask licensing@bl.uk for an item-specific written determination, quoting the IIIF statement Public Domain in most countries other than the UK.
Keep the complete image bundle Git-ignored until redistribution rights are clear.
Separate use of public-domain medieval text and factual collation from republication of the image files.

open_questions.question: Are digital images of Cotton MS Faustina B III currently available despite the component catalog's legacy unavailability statement?
next_steps: Use the parent record's current full-digital-coverage link and working IIIF manifest for access.
Retain the component's contrary sentence as historical catalog evidence until the British Library updates it.
notes: All 577 provider canvases were successfully cached and the ZIP passed integrity validation.

open_questions.question: Can the complete historical set of 124 U-M-linked page images be retrieved and audited through the current interface?
next_steps: Retry the advertised View Page routes in an interactive browser or through U-M-supported access.
Do not label the complete current image sequence available or unavailable until all sequences are checked.
notes: The current full text works, but automated page-view requests returned HTTP 403.

review_notes: The abstract Wilton Chronicle, Horstmann edition/frame, complete physical Cotton carrier, raw AJE artifact, shared U-M item, later phase-3 artifact, and both complete facsimiles are separate entities.
The supported phase-3 version_of relation is directional, qualified, and never same_as; phase-3 access and rights are independently recorded.
Current BL foliation governs discovery; Horstmann legacy foliation remains attached to edition and raw milestone evidence.
Horstmann relates to Black by direct manuscript transcription plus collation, not by copying Black alone.
The complete BL bundle maps whole-to-whole to the complete physical composite carrier; target presence remains ff. 199r-263r / canvases 407-535.
The complete Horstmann volume is cached; target work_portion is only the poem on printed pp. 1-111 / PDF pp. 15-125.
All eight cached objects explicitly declare target_work_presence. Supporting catalogs, metadata, scholarship, and rate information are absent rather than given invented work portions.
U-M NoC-US is modeled as public_domain in the United States with the canonical RightsStatements URI; the raw 2006 generic warning is a separate dated rights record.
Repository XML/markup rights remain unknown because the root Unlicense is framed for software and has ambiguous corpus-data scope.
Latin appendix material remains outside aje0623 target content even where complete source objects preserve it.
